old_claim.note: Sociology (Early) was used as a prerequisite for Demography.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains; Demography is scoped to population accounting/statistical practice with earlier evidentiary roots predating formal sociology as a field.

source_shape.source_locator: Historical overview identifying Graunt and early vital statistics as foundations of demography.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Demography is historically treated as a statistical population science emerging before mature sociology as a distinct discipline.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source shows demographic methods and data collection (censuses, birth/death records) as foundational, making a direct dependency on Early Sociology unnecessary and chronologically late for the core node.

ontology_before: Demography inherited a direct prerequisite from Early Sociology, forcing a later chronology gate for core historical claim.
ontology_after: Demography now depends on statistical prerequisites and historical population records, with sociology references removed from the core dependency chain.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: core dependency and chronology constraints were decoupled. Preserved: use of census/codified law as contextual enablers remains in place.
would_reject_if: A strong source showed demography requires formal early sociology frameworks for the node's scoped claim.
Demography were re-scoped to mean modern sociological population theory rather than population statistical practice.
short_reason: Demography is older than a modern sociological field; edge is non-essential and created a chronological inconsistency.
